_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper signals a critical vulnerability in AI-driven systems that could disrupt market dynamics by enabling unauthorized tool-use in BCI-LLM agents, potentially altering competitive landscapes in emerging digital markets.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of BCI-LLM agents could create new gatekeepers or disrupt existing digital markets by enabling direct neural interfaces to control tools and services.

items.title: Trajectory Geometry of Transformer Representations Across Layers
items.abstract: Understanding how transformer representations evolve across layers, not merely what they encode, remains an open problem in mechanistic interpretability. We recast the transformer forward pass as a discrete population trajectory through a high-dimensional representation manifold, drawing on geometric tools from computational neuroscience. Rather than probing for pre-specified features, we characterize trajectory geometry using five metrics computed directly in the ambient space: trajectory length, curvature, a semantic convergence index, layerwise cosine similarity, and representational stability. Across three model families (GPT-2, TinyLlama, Qwen2.5) and five controlled prompt families, we report four findings. First, semantically related prompts converge significantly in middle-to-late layers (peak CI 0.41--0.58, p<0.001, Mann-Whitney U), consistent with attractor-like dynamics. Second, reasoning tasks produce trajectories of greater curvature than lexical variations (0.71--0.83 rad vs. 0.27--0.31 rad), suggesting curvature encodes computational complexity. Third, ambiguous tokens exhibit trajectory bifurcation with up to 5.6x representational separation by the final layer, abse

_analysis.summary: This paper studies how AI language models (transformers) process information across their layers, modeling their internal workings as a path through a high-dimensional space. It introduces five new ways to measure how these paths change, like trajectory length and curvature, to understand how AI models organize and refine information without pre-defining what to look for.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Reveals how AI models' internal mechanics could influence their dominance in markets like cloud computing or AI services, where performance gaps drive competitive advantages.
_analysis.market_signal: AI model efficiency and interpretability breakthroughs may accelerate consolidation in AI infrastructure markets, favoring firms with superior internal architectures.

items.title: Internalizing Geometric Law: Learning from Solver Residuals for Precision-Critical Generation
items.abstract: Large Language Models frequently hallucinate in precision-critical domains such as technical diagramming and mechanical design, where outputs must satisfy strict geometric constraints. We study open-ended geometric synthesis from natural language: translating free-form descriptions into precise constructions whose entities must simultaneously satisfy dozens of interacting constraints. To make this tractable, we release PyGeoX, a programmable geometric DSL that compiles declarative constraints into a differentiable loss, and PyGeoX-Bench, a stratified suite of 300 problems with per-constraint verifiable rewards. Using PyGeoX as a verifier, we identify a failure mode we call Outlier Gradient Masking: under global-norm rewards (any scheme that aggregates residuals through a single norm, for example, $\exp(-\mathrm{MSE})$), a single outlier constraint can nullify the learning signal across all others. To address this, we propose Saturating Additive Rewards (SAR), which decompose the reward into bounded per-constraint terms, preserving partial progress and ensuring consistent gradients even under severe violations. Against MSE-based rewards, the natural baseline for geometry solvers, SA

_analysis.summary: This paper addresses a key limitation of Large Language Models (LLMs)—their tendency to produce inaccurate outputs in precision-critical tasks like technical diagramming or mechanical design, where strict geometric constraints must be met. The authors introduce PyGeoX, a programming tool that converts natural language descriptions into precise geometric constructions by enforcing dozens of interacting constraints, and PyGeoX-Bench, a benchmark of 300 problems to test these systems.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights the growing capability of AI systems to handle specialized, high-stakes tasks, which could reduce dependence on dominant platforms or tools in technical and design markets.
_analysis.market_signal: The development of AI systems capable of precise geometric synthesis signals a potential shift in markets for technical design software, where specialized tools may face competition from AI-driven alternatives.

items.title: Self-Paced Curriculum Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Superbike Racing in Simulation
items.abstract: Autonomous Racing has seen remarkable progress through deep Reinforcement Learning (RL), primarily for four-wheeled vehicles. However, motorbikes introduce substantially greater complexity due to the need to manage balance and lean angle, in addition to more reactive steering and throttle control, and a smaller weight. In this work, we present a framework for training an autonomous agent to race a superbike in VRider SBK, a physics-accurate Unity-based motorbike simulator. Our approach integrates Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) with Self-Paced curriculum Deep reinforcement Learning (SPDL), which dynamically generates progressively more challenging tasks based on the agent's performance, without requiring manual curriculum design. The agent's state space comprises proprioceptive features extended with lean-angle history, along with global track features via course points. The reward signal is shaped to encourage progress along the track while penalizing instability-inducing behaviors specific to two-wheeled dynamics. Preliminary experimental results demonstrate that SPDL outperforms SAC alone in training efficiency, lap time, and driving stability across multiple tracks and motorbike models

_analysis.summary: This academic paper presents a framework using reinforcement learning (a type of AI training where agents learn by trial and error) to train an autonomous agent to race a motorbike in a high-fidelity simulator. The approach combines Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), a reinforcement learning algorithm, with Self-Paced Curriculum Deep Reinforcement Learning (SPDL) to handle the complex dynamics of motorbike racing, such as balance and lean angle.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights advancements in autonomous systems that could disrupt industries reliant on human expertise, signaling potential shifts in competitive dynamics for AI-driven technologies.
_analysis.market_signal: Autonomous AI systems in high-stakes physical domains (e.g., racing) may accelerate the adoption of AI in logistics, delivery, and transportation, intensifying competition in these sectors.

items.title: End-to-End Training for Discrete Token LLM based TTS System
items.abstract: Recent state-of-the-art (SOTA) text-to-speech (TTS) systems typically adopt a cascaded pipeline consisting of a speech tokenizer, an autoregressive large language model (LLM), and a diffusion based flow-matching (FM) model, with these components trained independently. In this paper, we propose a fully end-to-end (E2E) optimization framework that unifies the training of the speech tokenizer, LLM, FM model, and an additional reward model (RM). Specifically, we first jointly optimize the tokenizer using multi-task objectives derived from reconstruction for FM, next-token prediction for LLM, and multi recognition task for RM. This joint training encourages the discrete speech token space to capture acoustically and semantically salient information that is better tailored to TTS. We then further optimize the LLM using downstream reconstruction and recognition by FM and RM, which reduces inference-time mismatch and steers the LLM toward more preferred generations. Experimental results show that our E2E framework consistently outperforms cascaded baselines. On the Seed-TTS-Eval benchmark, our system achieves a word error rate (WER) of 0.78% and 1.56%, a new SOTA result with a 0.6B-paramet

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes a new way to build text-to-speech (TTS) systems by training all parts of the system together (end-to-end) instead of separately. The authors combine a speech tokenizer, a large language model (LLM), a diffusion-based flow-matching model, and a reward model into a single training process to improve performance.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This research signals potential shifts in the AI infrastructure market by demonstrating how unified training frameworks could reduce barriers to entry for new TTS competitors, challenging existing dominant providers.
_analysis.market_signal: End-to-end training frameworks may lower costs and complexity for AI voice synthesis, enabling new entrants to compete with established players like Google, Amazon, or Microsoft in the TTS and AI voice markets.

items.title: Trustworthy Smart Fabs via Professional Proxies: Scaling Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) through Industrial Data Spaces
items.abstract: The convergence of the 2026 European Union Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) framework, Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD), and Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) introduce a severe governance bottleneck for advanced semiconductor manufacturing facilities ("Smart Fabs"). Regulatory compliance demands have surpassed the capacity of manual corporate reporting, creating a direct conflict between multi-stakeholder transparency and corporate data privacy. This paper addresses this challenge by introducing a zero-trust socio-technical orchestration framework that operationalizes a six-layer SSbD reference architecture within trustworthy industrial data spaces. We propose a shift from reactive automation to autonomous governance through "Professional Proxies"-role-based agentic workflows executing within hardware-isolated trust zones. Structured as an interoperable network protocol stack, the framework coordinates an automated, five-step "relay race" between Facility, Process Engineering, and Finance proxy teams to align factory-floor yield models with macro-level sustainability mandates. By executing Virtual Metrology (VM) predictions and Federated Machin

_analysis.summary: This academic paper proposes a 'zero-trust socio-technical orchestration framework' to help advanced semiconductor manufacturing facilities ('Smart Fabs') comply with new EU regulations like the Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) framework, Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD), and Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM). The framework aims to balance multi-stakeholder transparency with corporate data privacy, addressing a growing governance bottleneck in digital markets.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: The paper highlights regulatory pressures on semiconductor manufacturing (a critical digital infrastructure sector) that could influence market structure and competitive dynamics, particularly around data governance and interoperability.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals that regulatory compliance in digital infrastructure (e.g., semiconductor manufacturing) is becoming increasingly complex, potentially creating new barriers to entry or favoring firms with advanced data-sharing capabilities.
key_entities: Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) framework

items.title: The Injection Paradox: Brand-Level Suppression in Safety-Trained LLM Recommendations via RAG Context Injection
items.abstract: We present a reproducible failure mode of safety training in RAG-based LLM recommendation -- the Injection Paradox -- in which prompt injections embedded in retrieved documents backfire against the attacker, suppressing the target brand below the injection-free baseline. In safety-trained Claude models, documents containing prompt injections suffer a sharp drop in recommendation rate, and this suppression propagates beyond the injected document to unmodified documents of the same brand. In Claude Opus 4.6, the target brand drops from a 54% baseline to zero top-2 recommendations across all 50 trials, even though only 1 of 4 brand documents in the corpus contains an injection. The directional pattern is reproduced in counterfactual experiments and across three brands. A contrasting result across the GPT models tested, where the same injection instead increases recommendations, suggests model-family differences in how injection-like context affects recommendation behavior. These findings raise the technical possibility of a reverse-attack scenario in which an adversary embeds injections in a competitor's documents to suppress the competitor's brand via safety-sensitive model behavior.

_analysis.summary: This paper uncovers a new failure mode in AI safety training called the *Injection Paradox*, where prompt injections (malicious inputs designed to manipulate AI behavior) in retrieved documents unintentionally suppress the recommendation rates of targeted brands, even for unmodified documents from the same brand. In tests with safety-trained AI models like Claude, brands targeted by injections saw their recommendation rates drop sharply—from 54% to 0% in one case—revealing unintended consequences of safety measures in AI systems.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights how AI safety mechanisms can inadvertently distort competition by suppressing certain brands, raising concerns about fairness and market access in AI-driven recommendation systems.
_analysis.market_signal: AI safety training may introduce systemic biases that could entrench or disrupt market power in digital recommendation markets, potentially favoring incumbents or creating new barriers to entry.

items.title: MASS: Deep Research for Social Sciences with Memory-Augmented Social Simulation
items.abstract: Deep Research agents powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) have exhibited extraordinary potential in automated paper writing tasks. However, existing systems rely heavily on literature retrieval and synthesis through internet and local knowledge bases, often resulting research in lacking insight and creativity in social science. To address this issue, we propose "Memory-Augmented Social Simulation (MASS)", an innovative paradigm that leverages highly realistic and research-oriented social simulations to enhance the creativity and empirical founding of LLMs-generated research. Specifically, MASS integrates three core components: dynamic goal-path planning with multi-level social norm restraint to guide the simulation, a multi-disciplinary behavior dataset for agent memory cold-start, and a structured forgetting mechanism inspired by the Ebbinghaus curve. Together, these ensure simulation authenticity and provide a robust empirical foundation for generating innovative scholarly papers. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, showing a 6.81\% improvement in generation overall quality over foundation LLMs and 17.19\% gain in Insight over strong baselines.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces MASS (Memory-Augmented Social Simulation), a new AI system that uses realistic social simulations to help Large Language Models (LLMs) generate more creative and empirically grounded research in social sciences. Unlike traditional AI research tools that rely on existing literature, MASS simulates social interactions to improve the depth and originality of automated research.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: MASS demonstrates how AI agents can autonomously generate high-quality research, potentially reducing reliance on traditional academic and market research firms, which could disrupt competition in knowledge-intensive markets.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven research automation may lower barriers to entry for firms or researchers, challenging incumbents in knowledge markets and altering competitive dynamics in digital research services.

items.title: Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models
items.abstract: EEG foundation-model releases are usually audited one endpoint at a time: raw-reconstruction, membership inference, identity linkage, or DP-SGD on the downstream head. We audit the same released embeddings under all four endpoints jointly, on BIOT, LaBraM, and EEGPT, and show that each single-endpoint audit clears releases that still leak spectral attributes. The decisive evidence is a cross-encoder transfer audit: a single ridge attribute decoder learned from one frozen encoder transfers, via a fitted linear bridge, to held-out-subject test splits of every other encoder, with subject-disjoint matched-control 95% CI lower bound at least 0.081 across all six BIOT/LaBraM/EEGPT directions. We prove a sufficient condition: two encoders sharing a nontrivial attribute-coordinate projector overlap beta admit a chained ridge bridge attacker with centered-gain lower bound sqrt(beta/(1+tau^2)) - eps_br - rho_0, and back-solve beta in [0.008, 0.198]. To turn the joint audit into a deployment-readable decision rule we introduce an audit-endpoint disagreement score (AEDS), prove sufficient conditions for its positivity, and bootstrap-calibrate it per cell; AEDS is positive in all eight matched-

_analysis.summary: This academic paper audits three EEG (electroencephalogram) foundation models—BIOT, LaBraM, and EEGPT—by testing them across four privacy-related endpoints: raw-reconstruction, membership inference, identity linkage, and DP-SGD (Differential Privacy Stochastic Gradient Descent) on downstream tasks. The study finds that while each individual audit suggests the models are safe, a combined 'cross-encoder transfer audit' reveals that spectral attributes (e.g., brainwave patterns) can still be leaked, even when models appear compliant under single-endpoint tests. A 'ridge attribute decoder' (a simple linear model) trained on one model’s embeddings can predict attributes in other models, indicating systemic privacy risks in released embeddings.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on EEG data privacy, its methodology and findings highlight systemic risks in foundation model releases that could inform broader digital competition policy, particularly around data aggregation, interoperability, and gatekeeper obligations.
_analysis.market_signal: Foundation models with embedded data leakage risks may exacerbate competitive harms by enabling downstream actors to exploit sensitive user data, potentially undermining trust in AI-driven markets.

items.title: Culturally-Adapted Red-Teaming Across East and Southeast Asian Contexts: A Methodological and Comparative Analysis
items.abstract: Multilingual safety evaluation of large language models (LLMs) has predominantly relied on direct translation (DT) of English benchmarks into target languages - an approach that converts surface-level linguistic form while failing to reflect the cultural context embedded in threat scenarios, social norms, and legal frameworks. We construct paired DT and culturally-adapted (CA) datasets via 1:1 seed matching for four languages - Korean (KO), Japanese (JA), Thai (TH), and Khmer (KM) - and compare Attack Success Rate (ASR) and Cultural Realism scores across four open-source LLM. CA prompts yield Delta-ASR > 0 across all 16 language x model combinations (mean +9.3 pp), and DT-based evaluation underestimates risk in 44 of 48 category x language combinations. Language-level analysis reveals that the distribution of threat forms is heterogeneous across languages. Cultural Realism analysis further shows that DT Cultural Depth (C3) scores remain consistently below 1.0 out of 3.0 across all four languages (mean 0.17), whereas CA scores reach up to 2.51, indicating that direct translation produces inputs systematically divergent from those encountered in real-world multicultural settings. The

_analysis.summary: This academic paper critiques the current method of testing AI safety in non-English languages by simply translating English benchmarks, which ignores cultural differences in social norms and legal frameworks. It introduces a new approach using culturally-adapted (CA) prompts in Korean, Japanese, Thai, and Khmer, showing that these yield different results in AI safety evaluations compared to direct translations (DT).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Highlights how cultural adaptation in AI safety testing reveals gaps in global AI governance, which could influence regulatory expectations for multilingual AI systems and their market dominance.
_analysis.market_signal: Signals growing demand for culturally nuanced AI safety evaluations, potentially creating new markets for localized AI compliance tools and services.

items.title: Reliable to Expressive: A Curriculum for Rubric-Following Safety Judges
items.abstract: Safety judges are increasingly deployed to evaluate model outputs against evolving criteria, yet recent meta-evaluation work shows they remain brittle under prompt and rubric variation, with false negative-rate swings of up to 0.24 reported for stylistic perturbations alone. We argue that safety judgment is fundamentally a rubric-following problem: a robust judge must apply the given evaluation criteria consistently across rubric formulations rather than memorize one specific template. We propose a training strategy that combines (i) instance-conditioned dynamic rubrics generated from prompt-response-label triples to expose the judge to the variability of evaluation criteria, and (ii) a reliable-to-expressive curriculum that begins with clean fixed-rubric supervision and progressively introduces noisier dynamic-rubric data. We evaluate on a single human-labeled set under three contrasting rubric prompts (HarmBench-style, ShieldGemma-style, and a domain-specific rubric). Our 12B curriculum judge achieves 94.12-94.88% accuracy across the three rubrics with a cross-rubric range of only 0.76, outperforming general-purpose LLMs, dedicated safety classifiers, and reasoning-oriented judge

_analysis.summary: This paper examines the reliability of 'safety judges'—AI systems that evaluate other AI models' outputs against specific criteria (called 'rubrics'). It finds these judges are inconsistent when the wording of their evaluation criteria changes, sometimes failing to detect issues 24% of the time. The authors propose a training method to make these judges more adaptable to different rubric formats.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Shows how AI evaluation systems (used by platforms to moderate content or assess compliance with rules) can be manipulated or fail under variations in criteria, revealing vulnerabilities in digital market governance tools.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-driven content moderation and safety evaluation tools may become less reliable as platforms scale, creating gaps in enforcement that could distort competitive dynamics in digital markets.

items.title: Crop Recommendation and Agricultural Query Answering System Using Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks and Hybrid Retrieval Augmentation
items.abstract: This paper presents a unified system designed to support precision agriculture by integrating advanced weather prediction, crop recommendation, and a question-answering tool for farmers. We propose two deep learning models -- a Transformer-based Graph Neural Network and a Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network (STGCN) -- to forecast weather conditions for the next 30 days using data from 1,359 locations in Nepal. The STGCN outperforms the Transformer-based model in accuracy (MSE ~0.011 vs. 0.013), effectively modeling both spatial and temporal dependencies in climate data. These predictions are combined with static soil properties such as pH, moisture, and organic content to generate localized crop recommendations through a scoring algorithm that matches each crop's optimal growing conditions. Additionally, we develop a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) chatbot that leverages domain-specific agricultural documents to answer farmers' questions in natural language. The entire system is deployed via a mobile application, offering real-time suggestions and conversational support. User feedback confirms the system's usability and relevance, especially in rural settings where per

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a system using advanced machine learning models (a Transformer-based Graph Neural Network and a Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network, or STGCN) to improve weather forecasting and crop recommendations for farmers in Nepal. The STGCN model performed better at predicting weather conditions by analyzing spatial and temporal climate data from 1,359 locations.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on agricultural AI applications, it highlights the growing use of large-scale, data-intensive AI systems that could raise questions about data access, market power, and interoperability in digital agriculture markets.
_analysis.market_signal: The deployment of sophisticated AI models in niche sectors like agriculture signals broader trends toward AI-driven precision tools, which may influence competition in data-rich digital markets.

items.title: Unified Energy for Invariant and Independent Decoding in Diffusion Language Models
items.abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) enable parallel text generation by iteratively denoising a full sequence, offering attractive flexibility compared to auto-regressive (AR) decoding. However, existing methods fail to fully capture token relationships, leading to a performance gap relative to AR baselines, especially as the degree of parallelism increases. In this paper, we give a systematic analysis of the gap, identifying three key factors: (i) model capacity, (ii) dependency, and (iii) invariance. To address these issues, we first propose an invariant energy (Inv-E) together with an effective sampling-based estimator to handle the invariance issue. By further combining with the independent energy (Ind-E), we obtain a unified energy (Uni-E), that accounts for all these factors. Uni-E enjoys a unique advantage: it can be computed exactly without sampling-based partition estimation. Besides, Uni-E is model agnostic and can therefore be scaled to models of arbitrary size. We further prove that Uni-E can correct the distribution shift caused by dependency and invariance. Extensive experiments across Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) and Diffusion Large Language Models (DLLMs) demonstrat

_analysis.summary: This paper studies Diffusion Language Models (DLMs), a new way to generate text by refining a full sequence at once rather than word-by-word like traditional AI models. The authors find that DLMs struggle to match the performance of older methods because they don’t fully capture relationships between words, especially when generating text in parallel. They propose a fix called 'invariant energy' to improve how these models handle word dependencies.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: DLMs could disrupt the dominance of auto-regressive models (like those used by major AI labs) by enabling faster, more parallel text generation, potentially reshaping the AI infrastructure market.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of parallel text-generation models like DLMs may challenge the current market leaders in AI infrastructure and cloud-based language services.

items.title: SEF-CLGC at SemEval-2026 Task 11: Logical Notation Impact on Language Model Performance
items.abstract: This paper revisits our pipeline called Syllogistic Evaluation Framework-Common Logic Grammar Construction (SEF-CLGC). We combine formal logical notations with Small Language Models (SLMs) to evaluate reasoning performance on the SemEval-2026 Task 11 Subtask 1: Disentangling Content and Formal Reasoning in Large Language Models. Our experiments show that by relying solely on SLMs, trained on a combination of natural and symbolic languages, our best model achieves a content score of 27.80% on the task while significantly lowering the content bias in reasoning.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method called Syllogistic Evaluation Framework-Common Logic Grammar Construction (SEF-CLGC) that combines formal logical notations with small language models (SLMs) to improve reasoning performance. The approach reduces content bias in reasoning tasks, achieving a 27.80% content score on the SemEval-2026 Task 11 Subtask 1, which focuses on disentangling content and formal reasoning in large language models (LLMs).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper highlights advancements in AI reasoning that could influence how large tech platforms (potential gatekeepers under the EU's Digital Markets Act) design and deploy AI systems, potentially affecting market power dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved AI reasoning capabilities may enable smaller firms to compete more effectively with dominant AI providers, challenging existing market structures.
key_entities: SEF-CLGC (Syllogistic Evaluation Framework-Common Logic Grammar Construction)
SemEval (a benchmark for natural language processing tasks)

items.title: Decoding Pedestrian Crossing Intention from Egocentric Vision via Vision Language Models
items.abstract: Egocentric vision offers a first-person view of human perception and decision making, yet its potential for traffic-safety prediction remains underexplored. In this work, we study the decoding of pedestrian crossing intentions from short egocentric video clips. We approach this by formulating the task as a closed-ended visual question answering (VQA) problem and leveraging vision language models (VLMs) to predict the pedestrians' intent. We first benchmark three families of state-of-the-art VLMs in a zero-shot setting, finding that they achieve moderate gains over random guessing but exhibit limited higher-level traffic reasoning. Motivated by these findings, we further adapt VLMs to the target task using parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Our results show that the fine-tuned models substantially outperform their zero-shot counterparts and achieve a 9\% accuracy improvement over a specialized transformer-based baseline. Finally, we demonstrate that incorporating additional contextual cues, including ego motion, vehicle motion, and eye gaze, further improves predictive performance. In particular, the fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-2B model guided by eye gaze and ego motion achieves a 14.5% accur

_analysis.summary: This paper explores using vision-language models (VLMs) to predict whether pedestrians will cross the road by analyzing egocentric (first-person) video footage. The authors test three advanced VLMs in a zero-shot setting (without prior training on this specific task) and find they perform slightly better than random guessing but still have room for improvement.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While this research is innovative in AI applications for traffic safety, it does not directly address digital competition policy or market dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: This signals advancements in AI-driven perception systems that could eventually integrate into autonomous vehicles or smart infrastructure, potentially reshaping mobility markets.

items.title: Claw-R1: A Step-Level Data Middleware System for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
items.abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) has become an important post-training paradigm for turning LLMs from static chatbots into interactive agents, giving rise to representative applications such as OpenClaw. Existing work mainly focuses on policy optimization algorithms and training frameworks, but pays less attention to the full data lifecycle of agent-environment interactions, from data production to training consumption. To bridge this gap, we present Claw-R1, an interactive step-level data middleware system for agentic RL. Claw-R1 connects heterogeneous agent runtimes with RL training backends through two core components: a Gateway Server and a Data Pool. The Gateway Server captures multi-turn interaction steps through a unified LLM API entry point, while the Data Pool organizes them into step-level records consisting of prompt IDs, response IDs, rewards and other metadata. In our demo, users can interactively inspect live trajectories, examine the state, action, and reward of each step, curate data by quality and readiness, and configure training-ready batches for different downstream RL algorithms. Overall, Claw-R1 treats agent interaction traces as managed data assets rather

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Claw-R1, a new system designed to manage data from agentic reinforcement learning (RL), where AI models act as interactive agents rather than static chatbots. It focuses on the full data lifecycle of these agents, from how they interact with environments to how their data is used for training, addressing a gap in existing research.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: The middleware system could reduce barriers to entry for new AI agent developers, potentially disrupting the dominance of large AI platforms by enabling more efficient data collection and training pipelines.
_analysis.market_signal: The development of step-level data middleware for AI agents signals a shift toward more modular and competitive AI ecosystems, where smaller players can more easily build and deploy advanced agentic systems.

items.title: Steganography Without Modification: Hidden Communication via LLM Seeds
items.abstract: We demonstrate that widely deployed Large Language Model (LLM) inference stacks harbor a steganographic channel that requires no modification to model weights, sampling code, or output distributions. The channel exploits a structural property of deterministic decoding: pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) used in inverse-transform sampling produce a seed-dependent sequence of token-level probability intervals that can be reconstructed from the generated text alone. A sender encodes a secret message in the PRNG seed before generation; a receiver reconstructs the intervals and recovers the seed, and thus the hidden payload, by exhaustive search over the seed space. We formalize two operational modes. In the known-prompt setting, sender and receiver share the prompt, enabling exact interval reconstruction and perfect seed recovery via forced alignment. In the unknown-prompt setting, only the generated text is available; approximate interval reconstruction combined with a maximum-hit-count scoring strategy still permits reliable recovery from sufficiently long outputs. Extensive experiments across six model families and five heterogeneous text domains show that, in the known-prompt

_analysis.summary: This paper reveals a hidden communication channel in widely used Large Language Models (LLMs) that allows secret messages to be sent without altering the model's code or weights. The method exploits how LLMs generate text using pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs), where the initial 'seed' used for generation can encode hidden information that is later decoded from the output text alone.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates a novel way to exploit LLM inference stacks that could undermine trust in AI systems, potentially raising concerns about platform accountability and the security of AI-driven services.
_analysis.market_signal: Highlights vulnerabilities in AI infrastructure that could impact the reliability of AI-powered services, signaling the need for stronger safeguards in AI deployment.

items.title: From USD Scenes to Knowledge Graphs: Zero-Shot Ontology Grounding with LLMs
items.abstract: Constructing knowledge graphs from 3D simulation scenes is essential for robot task reasoning, but the key bottleneck, grounding scene objects to formal ontology classes, still relies on manually curated dictionaries that are brittle and do not generalize across assets. We investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can automate this grounding step for Universal Scene Description (USD) scenes as a zero-shot, training-free alternative. On a kitchen scene (125 objects) with SOMA-HOME Ontology, LLMs achieve 90-96% exact-match accuracy with descriptive names and 49-89% with abbreviated names, substantially outperforming dictionary and embedding baselines. Under fully opaque names, context-augmented prompting recovers up to 48%. Feature ablation reveals that LLMs primarily exploit semantic cues in the scene graph (sibling names and parent paths); anonymizing these cues reduces accuracy to 0-6%, while geometry alone yields only 4-17%.

_analysis.summary: This academic paper explores using large language models (LLMs) to automatically link objects in 3D simulation scenes (like those in robotics) to formal ontology classes (structured knowledge frameworks) without prior training. The study finds that LLMs can achieve high accuracy (90-96%) when object names are descriptive, but performance drops (49-89%) with abbreviated names, highlighting challenges in generalization.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how LLMs can automate knowledge-intensive tasks, signaling potential for AI to disrupt industries reliant on manual data curation, which may affect competitive dynamics in data-driven markets.
_analysis.market_signal: LLMs' ability to automate ontology grounding could reduce barriers to entry in markets requiring structured data integration, such as robotics or AI-driven automation.

items.title: Late-Layer Fusion is Enough: Dual-Path Vision Token Routing for Multimodal Large Language Models under Visual Saturation
items.abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) commonly inherit the deep, symmetric Transformer backbone designed for unimodal text modeling, and apply the same computation uniformly to image and language tokens. This design overlooks a key modality asymmetry: image and text tokens differ substantially in information density, redundancy, and required reasoning depth. Through a layer-wise analysis of LLaVA-1.5, we observe that vision tokens tend to saturate in the middle layers. Specifically, text-to-image attention decreases from 0.68 at layer 0 to 0.07 by layer 4, and stabilizes near 0.04 after layer 18, whereas text tokens continue to benefit from deep semantic processing. These findings suggest a mismatch between architectural symmetry and depth-asynchronous modality evolution, resulting in redundant visual computation and possible drift in perceptual representations during deep task-specific adaptation. Motivated by this, we propose Dual-Path Vision Token Routing (DPVR), a modality-asymmetric routing framework for efficient MLLMs. Its core instantiation, DPVR-LF (Late-Layer Fusion), routes vision tokens at the saturation point into a one-layer trainable side branch, runs a thirteen-l

_analysis.summary: This academic paper examines how multimodal large language models (MLLMs)—AI systems that process both images and text—waste computational resources by treating image and text inputs equally. The study finds that image data ('vision tokens') becomes less useful after the middle layers of the AI model, suggesting that future AI designs could save energy and improve efficiency by focusing processing power on text tokens in later stages.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This research reveals inefficiencies in AI model design that could lower barriers to entry for new competitors, potentially disrupting the dominance of large AI model providers by enabling more efficient and cost-effective alternatives.
_analysis.market_signal: More efficient AI model architectures may reduce the competitive advantage of well-funded incumbents, fostering innovation and competition in the AI market.

items.title: Unveiling Privacy Risks in Multi-modal Large Language Models: Task-specific Vulnerabilities and Mitigation Challenges
items.abstract: Privacy risks in text-only Large Language Models (LLMs) are well studied, particularly their tendency to memorize and leak sensitive information. However, Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs), which process both text and images, introduce unique privacy challenges that remain underexplored. Compared to text-only models, MLLMs can extract and expose sensitive information embedded in images, posing new privacy risks. We reveal that some MLLMs are susceptible to privacy breaches, leaking sensitive data embedded in images or stored in memory. Specifically, in this paper, we (1) introduce MM-Privacy, a comprehensive dataset designed to assess privacy risks across various multi-modal tasks and scenarios, where we define Disclosure Risks and Retention Risks. (2) systematically evaluate different MLLMs using MM-Privacy and demonstrate how models leak sensitive data across various tasks, and (3) provide additional insights into the role of task inconsistency in privacy risks, emphasizing the urgent need for mitigation strategies. Our findings highlight privacy concerns in MLLMs, underscoring the necessity of safeguards to prevent data exposure. Our dataset and code can be found here.

_analysis.summary: This academic paper examines new privacy risks in Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs), which process both text and images, finding they can leak sensitive information embedded in images or stored in memory. Unlike traditional text-only models, MLLMs introduce unique vulnerabilities that could expose private data, raising concerns about data security and misuse.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: The paper highlights how MLLMs' ability to process and leak sensitive visual data could exacerbate data concentration risks, potentially reinforcing the dominance of firms controlling large datasets.
_analysis.market_signal: The rise of MLLMs signals a shift toward more integrated data processing, which may intensify competition for data assets and raise barriers to entry for smaller firms.

items.title: A Regret Minimization Framework on Preference Learning in Large Language Models
items.abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has enabled progress on reasoning-intensive tasks by relying on task-specific verifiers that provide automated correctness signals. However, many realistic language tasks are difficult to equip with reliable verifiers, motivating a growing reliance on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). In this setting, we argue that a closer examination of how human feedback should be interpreted is essential. We introduce Regret-based Preference Optimization $(\textbf{RePO})$, which reframes RLHF through $\textit{regret minimization}$ rather than reward maximization. Human preferences are often shaped by $\textit{prospective}$ anticipation of outcomes and $\textit{counterfactual}$ comparisons to alternative behaviors, rather than by immediate, outcome-independent utility. $\textbf{RePO}$ captures this structure by modeling preferences as behavior-conditioned assessments of relative suboptimality. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and human preference datasets demonstrate consistent performance gains, indicating that $\textbf{RePO}$ is an effective and human-aligned approach for training large language models.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Regret-based Preference Optimization (RePO), a new method for training large language models (LLMs) using human feedback. Instead of traditional reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), RePO focuses on minimizing 'regret'—the difference between desired and actual outcomes—to improve model performance on reasoning tasks where reliable automated verification is difficult.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work signals a shift in how AI models are trained, which could reduce reliance on proprietary data and feedback loops dominated by a few large tech firms, potentially lowering barriers to entry in AI markets.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in preference learning may democratize AI development, challenging the dominance of firms with large-scale human feedback datasets.

items.title: Autonomous Incident Resolution at Hyperscale: An Agentic AI Architecture for Network Operations
items.abstract: Cloud network infrastructure at hyperscale presents unique operational challenges where traditional human-driven incident response cannot keep pace with the volume, velocity, and complexity of failures. This paper presents an agentic AI architecture for autonomous incident resolution in large-scale network operations. Our system employs a multi-agent orchestration framework where specialized AI agents collaborate to detect, diagnose, and remediate network incidents without human intervention. We describe the architectural principles, including hierarchical agent decomposition, skills-based tool invocation via standardized protocols, structured knowledge encoding from operational runbooks, progressive autonomy with safety boundaries, and closed-loop verification. The architecture has been deployed in production at a major cloud provider, demonstrating that agentic AI systems can achieve autonomous resolution rates exceeding 90% for common incident categories while maintaining safety guarantees through layered authorization and rollback mechanisms. We discuss design tradeoffs, failure modes, and lessons learned from operating autonomous AI agents at scale.

_analysis.summary: This paper proposes an agentic AI architecture—using specialized AI agents working together—to autonomously detect, diagnose, and fix network failures in large-scale cloud systems without human input. The system uses a hierarchical structure where different AI agents handle specific tasks, aiming to match the speed and complexity of modern cloud infrastructure.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how AI-driven automation in cloud operations could reduce barriers to entry for smaller firms by lowering reliance on human labor and legacy infrastructure, potentially reshaping competitive dynamics in cloud services.
_analysis.market_signal: AI-powered autonomous systems may accelerate hyperscale cloud adoption, intensifying competition between cloud providers and challenging incumbents like AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud.

items.title: ComplexConstraints and Beyond: Expert Rubrics for RLVR
items.abstract: As LLM capabilities advance rapidly, the evaluation methods used to assess them increasingly lag behind. Traditional benchmarks relied on programmatic verification of narrow, surface-level constraints, but real-world instruction following and agentic tasks demand assessment of nuanced, context-dependent behaviors that resist simple scripted checks. We present a systematic analysis of expert-curated rubric-based evaluation as an alternative paradigm, drawing on empirical evidence from two domains: complex instruction following and enterprise agentic tasks. We first articulate five design principles for constructing high-quality rubrics, including Maximum Viable Atomicity, intent-aware criterion design, and iterative LLM-judge calibration. To validate these principles, we introduce ComplexConstraints, a new expert-curated instruction-following dataset in which each prompt is paired with 10-40 atomic rubric criteria. We demonstrate that these expert rubrics are not only better evaluation instruments but also highly effective training signals: training on approximately 1,000 ComplexConstraints examples yields +15.5% improvement for a 4B-parameter model and +12.2% for a 235B-parameter m

_analysis.summary: This paper argues that traditional ways of testing AI models (like simple automated checks) are outdated because they can't capture how well AI follows complex, real-world instructions or performs agentic tasks (tasks that involve acting in an environment). It proposes using detailed, expert-designed rubrics (grading guidelines) to evaluate AI more accurately, showing examples from complex instruction-following and enterprise agentic tasks.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights how AI capabilities are outpacing evaluation methods, which could lead to market power shifts if firms exploit gaps in oversight to dominate niche AI-driven tasks.
_analysis.market_signal: AI models with advanced instruction-following and agentic capabilities may enable new entrants to compete in enterprise software and automation markets, challenging incumbents like Microsoft and Google.

items.title: Optimizing Energy-based Neural Network Training with Coherent Ising Machine
items.abstract: While Ising machines serve as advanced physical solvers for the Ising model,enabling applications in combinatorial optimization and neural network training,their scalability for large-scale neural networks remains constrained by hardware connectivity limitations and suboptimal training methodologies. In this work,we leverage a Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) to train an energy-based neural network using Equilibrium Propagation, achieving performance comparable to existing software-based implementations. We further enhance the algorithm by integrating the Adam optimizer to solve for the ground state of a Hopfield energy network, significantly improving convergence speed and solution accuracy. Additionally, we demonstrate the scalability of our approach across deeper network architectures and convolutional operations. Our results highlight the potential of CIM dynamics as a scalable platform for training complex neural networks, offering a pathway toward energy-efficient implementations via analog circuits, optoelectronics, or integrated photonics. This work establishes a novel physical framework for next-generation AI hardware development.

_analysis.summary: This paper explores using a Coherent Ising Machine (CIM), a specialized hardware solver for optimization problems, to train energy-based neural networks more efficiently. By combining CIM with the Equilibrium Propagation training method and the Adam optimizer, the authors achieve results comparable to traditional software-based approaches, addressing scalability challenges in large-scale neural network training.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper advances neural network training efficiency, it does not directly address digital competition policy or market dynamics.
_analysis.market_signal: This research signals potential improvements in AI training hardware that could lower barriers to entry for AI model development, indirectly affecting competitive dynamics in AI markets.

items.title: Graph2Idea:Retrieval-Augmented Scientific Idea Generation with Graph-Structured Contexts
items.abstract: Generating novel, feasible, and high-quality research ideas is an important yet challenging task in scientific discovery.Recent Large Language Model (LLM)-based methods often ground idea generation with retrieved literature, but the retrieved evidence is usually provided as flat text, such as titles, abstracts, or summaries. Such flat contexts may contain redundant or weakly relevant information, while making cross-paper relations among problems, methods, mechanisms, and findings difficult to identify and trace.To address this challenge, we propose Graph2Idea, a knowledge graph-guided framework for retrieval-augmented scientific idea generation.Graph2Idea first retrieves papers according to the input topic, transforms them into structured knowledge triples, and dynamically constructs a target-centered knowledge graph to make literature relations explicit.It then extracts compact graph-derived contexts that retain target-relevant relational evidence while reducing noisy textual input.Based on these contexts, a two-stage generation process first identifies promising research directions and then guides the LLM to synthesize candidate ideas from graph-grounded evidence.Experiments on a

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Graph2Idea, a framework that uses knowledge graphs (structured representations of relationships between concepts) to help generate scientific research ideas by organizing and retrieving information from academic literature more effectively than traditional flat-text methods. Large Language Models (LLMs) are currently used to assist idea generation, but they often struggle with irrelevant or redundant information in unstructured text.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper highlights the growing role of structured data and AI in scientific discovery, which could influence how digital platforms and AI systems are regulated, particularly around data access and interoperability.
_analysis.market_signal: Structured knowledge graphs and AI-driven idea generation may reduce reliance on dominant academic databases or platforms, potentially disrupting current market dynamics in scholarly publishing and AI tooling.

items.title: From Shortcuts to Reasoning: Robust Post-Training of Theory of Mind with Reinforcement Learning
items.abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM) is a must-acquire skill for modern foundation model systems to operate effectively and safely in the real world. Recent works have explored honing ToM via post-training; however, we show that such progress is confounded by a pervasive "shortcut" issue: tasks can reach up to 99% accuracy by simply exploiting spurious causal correlations, leading to a false sense of ToM. Motivated by this, we first develop a framework to systematically examine ToM datasets for shortcuts and provide guidance for future development. We find that questions reducible to pure state tracking, such as "belief," are especially shortcut-prone compared to mind questions, such as "intention," where reasoning beyond tracking is required. Using four shortcut-free datasets across three ToM contexts, we then comprehensively study whether Reinforcement Fine-Tuning with verifiable rewards and explicit reasoning chains, called Thinking-RFT, elevates ToM beyond Supervised Fine-Tuning, or SFT. Our key findings are as follows. First, Thinking-RFT effectively improves ToM in all scenarios, with a 6% improvement over SFT, particularly in complex higher-order reasoning, with a 10% improvement over SFT,

_analysis.summary: This paper examines how modern AI systems (called 'foundation models') can develop a skill called 'Theory of Mind' (ToM), which is their ability to understand others' perspectives. The authors find that current AI models often 'cheat' by relying on simple patterns in data rather than truly understanding, leading to overstated capabilities. They propose a framework to test for these shortcuts in AI training.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Shows AI systems can appear competent in key areas (like reasoning) without genuine capability, which could mislead competition authorities about market power in AI-driven services.
_analysis.market_signal: AI models may overstate their capabilities, potentially leading to overestimation of competitive threats or underestimation of risks in AI-dominated markets.

items.title: Context-Fractured Decomposition Attacks on Tool-Using LLM Agents: Exploiting Artifact Provenance Gaps
items.abstract: Tool-using LLM agents interact with the world through actions that persist state in artifacts (e.g., workspace files or logs). Consequently, jailbreak defenses must reason about cross-step composition rather than isolated text. Yet most existing attacks and defenses, including ``multi-turn'' jailbreaks such as Crescendo and Tree of Attacks,still assume a single contiguous conversation visible to the defender. This assumption breaks down in real agent pipelines, where enforcement is fragmented across tools, modules, and time, and where artifact provenance is often not tracked. We operationalize a deployment failure mode for tool-using LLM agents, the \emph{provenance gap}, and study reproducible triggers for it: \emph{Context-Fractured Decomposition} (CFD), a family of cross-context multi-step jailbreaks that preserve benign-looking intermediate artifacts from an early interaction and elicit harmful behavior much later, potentially in a different agent instance or workflow stage, via individually innocuous tool actions whose risk emerges only under delayed artifact-mediated composition. We instrument the failure mode with trace-level diagnostics and outline a verifiable mitigation d

_analysis.summary: This paper reveals a new type of security vulnerability in AI agents that use tools (like file systems or logs) to interact with the world. These agents can be tricked into harmful actions by exploiting gaps in how their actions are tracked across different steps and tools—a problem called 'artifact provenance gaps.' Most defenses against such attacks assume a single, continuous conversation, but real-world AI systems are fragmented, making them easier to exploit.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work highlights systemic risks in AI agent ecosystems that could undermine trust in AI-powered tools, potentially reshaping competitive dynamics in digital markets where AI agents are becoming critical infrastructure.
_analysis.market_signal: AI agents with unchecked vulnerabilities may accelerate consolidation among tech firms that control secure AI toolchains, reinforcing gatekeeper power.

items.title: Teach Multimodal Recommendation Model to See via Personalized Visual Extraction and Adaptive Learning
items.abstract: Multimodal sequential recommendation (MSR) incorporates textual and visual information to improve recommendation quality. However, recent studies and our empirical analysis show that visual features are often underutilized, thereby contributing far less than textual signals. We attribute this issue to two factors: insufficient visual representation learning (pretrained encoders fail to capture preference-relevant cues) and unbalanced visual-text optimization (textual features dominate the learning process). To address these issues, we propose Teach Multimodal Recommendation Model to See via Personalized Visual Extraction and Adaptive Learning (REVEAL), a plug-and-play framework that enhances visual representation learning and cross-modal optimization without modifying the original recommendation backbone. REVEAL consists of Feedback-Guided Visual Extraction (FVE), which refines prompt-guided visual extraction through task-level feedback, and Adaptive Visual Learning (AVL), which dynamically reweights visual learning to alleviate modality imbalance. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets and MSR backbones demonstrate that REVEAL consistently improves recommendation performance.

_analysis.summary: This academic paper examines multimodal sequential recommendation (MSR) systems, which combine text and visual data to improve personalized recommendations. It finds that visual features are often underused because pretrained visual encoders fail to capture user preferences and text dominates the learning process. The authors propose a method called 'Teach Multimodal Recommendation Model to See' (Teach-MSR) to better integrate visual cues into recommendations.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This paper highlights how dominant platforms may leverage text-heavy recommendation systems to maintain market power, potentially sidelining visual content providers or smaller competitors.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved multimodal recommendation systems could shift competitive dynamics in e-commerce, social media, and content platforms by enhancing personalization and user engagement.

items.title: Beyond FLOPs: Benchmarking Real Inference Acceleration of LLM Pruning under a GEMM-Centric Taxonomy
items.abstract: Pruning has emerged as a dominant paradigm for accelerating large language model (LLM) inference, spanning a broad spectrum of methods that remove computation across tokens, layers, heads, dimensions, and attention patterns. Despite sharing the same objective, these pruning approaches induce fundamentally different execution behaviors, causing realized speedups to depend heavily on hardware and kernel implementations. Consequently, the practical acceleration benefits of different pruning families remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a GEMM-centric taxonomy that reorganizes existing pruning methods according to the logical \textbf{M}, \textbf{N}, and \textbf{K} dimensions of general matrix multiplication (GEMM). Leveraging this abstraction, we build a unified benchmarking framework that enables implementation-consistent comparison across the pruning design space and systematically characterizes the acceleration--quality Pareto frontier. Our results show that static depth pruning remains the strongest Pareto-optimal baseline and stays closest to its theoretical acceleration upper bound in memory-bounded scenarios. During prefill, the frontier transitions from static d

_analysis.summary: This paper examines how different methods for speeding up large language models (LLMs) through 'pruning' (removing parts of the model to make it faster) lead to varying real-world performance depending on hardware and software optimizations. It introduces a new framework to categorize these pruning techniques based on how they interact with matrix multiplication (GEMM), a core computational process in LLMs.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This work reveals how technical optimizations in AI model efficiency can shift competitive advantages among AI infrastructure providers and cloud computing platforms, potentially altering market dynamics in AI services.
_analysis.market_signal: Optimized LLM inference methods could reduce barriers to entry for smaller AI firms, challenging the dominance of large cloud providers like AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure in AI infrastructure.
key_entities: GEMM (General Matrix Multiplication, a core computational process)

items.title: FlashMemory-DeepSeek-V4: Lightning Index Ultra-Long Context via Lookahead Sparse Attention
items.abstract: Conventional LLMs keep the full KV cache loaded during decoding, causing a severe GPU memory bottleneck for ultra-long context serving. In this report, we propose Lookahead Sparse Attention (LSA), a novel inference paradigm powered by a Neural Memory Indexer built upon the DeepSeek-V4 architecture. Rather than passively attending to all historical tokens, LSA proactively predicts future context demands and preserves only the query-critical KV chunks in the GPU memory. Crucially, we instantiate this architecture via a backbone-free decoupled training strategy. By formulating the indexer as a standard dual-encoder architecture, we train it independently using standard retrieval training frameworks without ever loading the massive backbone model into GPU memory. We demonstrate that this "less is more" paradigm significantly maximizes serving efficiency while acting as an effective attention denoiser in tasks that rely on long-term global memory. Across primary long-context evaluation suites (e.g., LongBench-v2, LongMemEval, and RULER), FM-DS-V4 compresses the average physical KV cache footprint down to merely 13.5% of the full-context baseline, while consistently preserving or slightl

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces a new method called Lookahead Sparse Attention (LSA) to improve how large language models (LLMs) handle very long conversations or documents. Instead of storing all past information in memory, LSA predicts which parts of the conversation will be most important for future responses and only keeps those, reducing the computing power needed. The approach is built on the DeepSeek-V4 architecture and uses a separate system to train the memory indexer without relying on a traditional backbone model.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: LSA's ability to drastically reduce memory and computational costs for ultra-long context LLMs could lower barriers to entry for smaller firms or open-source projects, potentially disrupting the dominance of large AI model providers by making advanced AI more accessible.
_analysis.market_signal: This technology signals a shift toward more efficient and scalable AI models, which may reduce the competitive moats of incumbents like OpenAI or Google by enabling new entrants to compete with lower infrastructure costs.

items.title: REFLECT: Intervention-Supported Error Attribution for Silent Failures in LLM Agent Traces
items.abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents now solve complex tasks through long plan-and-execution traces, yet the ability to locate errors in a completed traces still lags far behind, especially in the \emph{silent failure} regime. Existing approaches predict suspect steps via classifiers or LLM judges, or recover correct answers via retry, but none feed the intervention outcome back to \emph{refine the attribution itself}. We propose \methodname, a method that closes this gap by diagnosing a candidate error step, testing it through controlled replay with a diagnosis-specific patch, and using the verified outcome flip as contrastive evidence to refine the final attribution. Across four localization benchmarks spanning multi-hop reasoning across domains, \methodname achieves the highest localization accuracy among same-auditor methods across all four benchmarks, with the largest gains on structured tool-use traces, while providing actionable localization even when ground-truth answers are unavailable.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces REFLECT, a method to improve the reliability of large language model (LLM) agents by identifying and correcting 'silent failures'—errors in long task execution traces that go unnoticed. The approach involves diagnosing potential errors, testing fixes through controlled replays, and refining error attribution based on intervention outcomes.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Demonstrates how AI agents can autonomously detect and correct errors, signaling potential for AI-driven automation to disrupt markets where reliability and oversight are critical.
_analysis.market_signal: Advances in AI error correction could accelerate the deployment of autonomous agents, potentially reducing reliance on human oversight in digital markets and challenging incumbents' dominance in sectors like cloud services or enterprise software.

items.title: See More, Think Deeper: Query-Expanded Visual Evidence and Answer-Clue Guided Reflection for Long Video Understanding
items.abstract: Recent advances in Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have enabled performance on long-video understanding tasks. However, existing methods still face two key limitations: evidence acquisition often relies on a single search intent, and answer generation lacks an effective visual feedback mechanism. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{CoVER}, a Comprehensive Visual Evidence and Reflection framework for long-video understanding. CoVER enables Video-LLMs to \textbf{See More} by dynamically gathering query-expanded visual evidence, and \textbf{Think Deeper} by verifying draft answers with effective answer-specific visual feedback. Together, these mechanisms shift long-video understanding from answer-centric generation to evidence-centric and visually verifiable reasoning. Experimental results show that CoVER-7B substantially outperforms models with the same parameter scale and even surpasses state-of-the-art closed-source models on certain metrics.

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces **CoVER**, a new framework for Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) that improves long-video understanding by dynamically gathering more visual evidence and using a reflection mechanism to verify answers. Video-LLMs are AI systems that analyze video content, but existing methods struggle with limited evidence collection and lack of feedback loops.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This research highlights advancements in AI-powered video analysis, which could influence market dynamics for video platforms and AI services, particularly in terms of data access and model capabilities.
_analysis.market_signal: Improved Video-LLMs may intensify competition among AI-driven video analysis tools, potentially reshaping the market for video content understanding services.
key_entities: CoVER (Comprehensive Visual Evidence and Reflection framework)

items.title: Decoy-Calibrated Failure Audits for Language Models
items.abstract: Useful audits reveal not only how often a model fails, but also where its failures concentrate. An auditor may test many candidate explanations: long inputs, indirect questions, distracting evidence, or combinations of these factors. The risk is selection. The largest observed effect may reflect a real failure mode, or it may simply be the best result among many tried. We introduce Janus, a procedure for deciding when a proposed error explanation is credible enough to report. The goal is not to generate new explanations, but to decide which ones hold up. The auditor starts with a fixed model, a labeled evaluation set, and a frozen list of candidate explanations, which we call descriptors. Janus scores each descriptor by its error-rate lift, then compares real descriptors with fake ones that have the same frequencies but are randomly assigned to examples. A descriptor is confirmed only if it beats this decoy floor on the data used for discovery and then repeats on separate held-out data. In a controlled audit of multi-table lookup tasks, Janus identifies the planted failure, confirming long-chain descriptors and their interactions. The LLM often stops partway through the lookup chai

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces Janus, a method to audit language models (LMs) and determine whether observed failures are credible or just random noise from testing many explanations. It helps auditors decide which error explanations are robust enough to report, rather than just cherry-picking the most dramatic results.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Janus addresses the reliability of AI failure audits, which is critical for assessing potential harms (e.g., bias, misinformation) that could trigger competition or regulatory scrutiny of dominant AI systems.
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals growing sophistication in auditing AI systems, which may pressure regulators to enforce stricter oversight on AI-driven platforms and their market power.

items.title: Agent Economics: An Entropy-Controlled Pluralistic Alignment Framework for Preventing Artificial Hivemind in Autonomous Agents
items.abstract: This study proposes the Behavioral Protocol Framework (BPF), an entropy-controlled pluralistic alignment framework designed to address two critical challenges in autonomous agent economies: the hivemind effect arising from excessive strategic convergence among agents and the lack of transparency in autonomous decision-making processes. The proposed BPF consists of three core modules: Mentalizing-based Social Intelligence (MbSI) grounded in Theory of Mind (ToM), Pluralistic Alignment (PA), and a Verifiable Execution Kernel (VEK). These modules are organically integrated within a closed-loop architecture that governs the entire lifecycle of agent behavior, from decision-making and execution to verification and feedback. To evaluate the proposed framework, a simulation environment implemented in Python and a Streamlit-based user interface will be developed. Through empirical experimentation, the study aims to examine whether the entropy-control mechanism of the PA module can effectively preserve strategic diversity among agents and mitigate collective convergence, while the VEK module provides a comprehensive and transparent audit trail of the decision-making process. The anticipated

_analysis.summary: This paper introduces the Behavioral Protocol Framework (BPF), a system designed to prevent autonomous AI agents from forming a 'hivemind'—where too many agents converge on the same strategies, reducing diversity and transparency. The BPF uses three tools: Mentalizing-based Social Intelligence (MbSI, which helps agents understand each other like humans do), Pluralistic Alignment (PA, which ensures diverse decision-making), and a Verifiable Execution Kernel (VEK, which makes AI decisions transparent and auditable).
_analysis.relevance_explanation: This framework could disrupt digital markets by enabling AI agents to operate more independently and transparently, potentially reducing the dominance of large platforms that rely on opaque, centralized decision-making.
_analysis.market_signal: AI agents with pluralistic alignment may challenge the control of gatekeeper platforms by introducing more competitive and transparent decision-making in digital markets.

items.title: Personalization Meets Safety:Mechanisms,Risks,and Mitigations in Personalized LLMs
items.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled increasingly personalized interactions by adapting to users' preferences, contexts, and long-term histories. However, the mechanisms that enable personalization also expand the safety landscape in ways not systematically addressed by existing literature. Existing reviews typically focus either on personalization or safety, leaving their intersection largely unexplored. We present the first comprehensive, safety-aware review of personalized LLMs. We organize personalization along three dimensions-user representation, personalization paradigm, and evaluation-and introduce a unified taxonomy of safety risks. At the representation level, we analyze risks arising from diverse user representations. Across mainstream personalization paradigms, we delineate vulnerabilities inherent to prompting, retrieval augmentation, parameter fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), pruning, agent frameworks, and multimodal personalization, and synthesize mitigation strategies across the model lifecycle. Beyond these fine-grained risks, we characterize paradigm-agnostic safety risks arising from personalized adaptation. We further summarize

_analysis.summary: This academic paper examines how Large Language Models (LLMs) enable personalized interactions by learning from users' preferences and histories, but warns that these same mechanisms introduce new safety risks not fully addressed by current research. It provides the first comprehensive review of personalized LLMs, focusing on how personalization works, its potential dangers, and ways to mitigate those risks.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Highlights how personalized AI systems could concentrate data and market power in a few dominant AI providers, raising competition concerns about access to user data and model interoperability.
_analysis.market_signal: Personalized LLMs may accelerate the dominance of a few AI platforms that control vast amounts of user data, potentially creating new gatekeepers in digital markets.

items.title: EFX for Additive Chores: Nonexistence, Pareto Incompatibility, and Bi-Valued Existence
items.abstract: We consider the fair division problem of indivisible chores and resolve the long-standing open problem for the existence of EFX allocations with additive cost functions. We show that, even for tri-valued additive cost functions, for every $n\geq 4$, there exists an instance with $n$ agents where no EFX allocation exists. Our counterexample only uses three types of chores, which is also tight on the number of types, as an EFX allocation is known to exist for two types of chores. We then consider bi-valued instances. We show that, for every $n\geq 4$, there exists an instance with $n$ agents where every EFX allocation is not Pareto-optimal. This is also the first example showing the incompatibility of EFX and Pareto-optimality when the costs of items are positive: existing examples showing the incompatibility of EFX and Pareto-optimal exploit items with $0$ costs. Our result shows such an example exists even for bi-valued instances. The number of agents $n$ is also tight: for $n\leq 3$, it is known that EFX is compatible with Pareto-optimality. Finally, we also show that an EFX allocation is guaranteed to exist for $n=4$.

_analysis.summary: This paper tackles the fair division problem of assigning indivisible 'chores' (tasks or costs) among agents, proving that for four or more agents, no allocation exists that satisfies the *Envy-Free up to any item (EFX)* criterion—even when chores are grouped into three types. EFX is a fairness standard where no agent would prefer another’s bundle after removing any single item. The study also shows that for two types of chores, EFX allocations are always possible.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: While the paper focuses on fair division theory rather than digital markets, its insights into indivisible resource allocation could indirectly inform debates on algorithmic fairness in digital platforms (e.g., ad allocation, cloud resource management).
_analysis.market_signal: The paper signals the growing complexity of fairness in algorithmic systems, which may influence future regulation on AI-driven resource allocation in digital markets.

items.title: A Taxonomy of Real-World Asset Tokenization for Blockchain-Based Financial Infrastructure
items.abstract: Real-world asset (RWA) tokenization has emerged as a prominent application of blockchain technology, enabling off-chain financial and non-financial assets to be represented through blockchain-based instruments. However, deployed RWA systems remain difficult to compare because legal claims, custody arrangements, token mechanics, verification processes, and on-chain integrations are often described separately. This paper develops a systems-level taxonomy of RWA tokenization to classify how off-chain assets are legally, economically, and technically represented on-chain. Following an iterative taxonomy-development method, we organize twenty-three dimensions into five components: governance, asset structure, token properties, distributed ledger technology, and economy. We apply the taxonomy to twenty major RWA systems selected by market capitalization and compare their design choices across asset classes and implementation models. The classification shows that current RWA tokenization is predominantly implemented through hybrid architectures: blockchain tokens support representation, transfer control, redemption workflows, pricing, and composability, while core legal guarantees remain

_analysis.summary: This paper creates a framework to classify how real-world assets (like property or stocks) are turned into digital tokens on blockchain systems. It highlights the complexity of these systems, where legal rights, technical processes, and economic structures are often mixed together in different ways, making them hard to compare or regulate.
_analysis.relevance_explanation: Tokenization of real-world assets could reshape financial markets by enabling new intermediaries, altering market structure, and potentially challenging traditional gatekeepers like banks or stock exchanges.
_analysis.market_signal: The growth of RWA tokenization signals a shift toward decentralized financial infrastructure, which may disrupt existing financial intermediaries and create new competitive dynamics in digital markets.

items.title: The Winner's Bliss in Common-Value Auctions under Horizontal Differentiation
items.abstract: We study common-value auctions in which bidders have horizontally differentiated preferences. In a specific two-bidder parameterization, winning conveys good news about the object's value to the winner, a phenomenon we call the winner's bliss in contrast to the conventional winner's curse. Additional implications also differ from the conventional analysis.
